data.title: Lincoln Electric expects high single-digit % 2026 net sales growth as new price actions start in May
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lincoln Electric (LECO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We achieved solid results, led by record quarterly sales and adjusted EPS performance while also navigating heightened operating complexity from geopolitics and evolving trade negotiations." (President, CEO & Chairman Steven Hedlund)
  • "The global launch of our new RISE strategy was successful" and included "the U.S. launch of our elite customer program as part of our enterprise-wide Spotlight initiative," a Harris automated line that "triples the line's productivity," and "a new center-led process innovation function in welding consumables to accelerate our speed to market." (President, CEO & Chairman Hedlund)
  • "Our 10% higher price did not fully offset inflation in the quarter" and "we have already announced new price actions across our welding segments, which go into effect in early May." (President, CEO & Chairman Hedlund)
  • "Cash flows, while seasonally lower, were further affected by a temporary increase in inventory levels" tied to service levels under Spotlight and product migrations; "ROIC performance remained at top quartile levels at 21.5%." (President, CEO & Chairman Hedlund)
  • "Our first quarter sales increased approximately 12% to $1.121 billion" and "gross profit margin declined 80 basis points to 35.6%" with "price/cost...unfavorable 90 basis points in the quarter." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Gabriel Bruno)

Outlook

  • "We now expect net sales growth to be in the high single-digit percent range as compared to our initial assumption of mid-single-digit percent growth." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno)
  • "We have not changed our original volume growth assumption of a low single-digit percent growth rate" and "our organic sales mix is now expected to be 3/4 price at a mid-single-digit percent rate and 1 quarter volume." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno)
  • "We estimate the sales impact from the Middle East conflict to be $8 million to $10 million per quarter while the conflict persists" and said it is "split evenly between the Americas and International Welding segments." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno)
  • "I would expect price/cost neutral as we enter the third quarter" after May pricing actions, with "the full impact in the third quarter." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno)
  • Analysts’ estimates were provided, but the JSON fiscal-quarter format did not match the required Q1–Q4 validation rule, so comparisons were skipped.

Financial Results

  • "First quarter diluted earnings per share performance increased 18% to $2.47" and "on an adjusted basis, earnings per share increased 16% to $2.50." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno)
  • "We held our adjusted operating income margin steady year-over-year at 16.9%" and "adjusted operating income increased 11.5% to $189 million." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno)
  • "Americas Welding sales increased approximately 8%" and "we expect volumes to inflect to modest growth in the second quarter"; the company also guided "Americas Welding margin" to "perform in the mid-18% to mid-19% EBIT margin range for the remainder of the year." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno)
  • "International Welding segment sales increased approximately 4%" and "we now expect International Welding's margins performance to improve sequentially but remain in the 11% range until conditions improve in the Middle East." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno)
  • "First quarter sales increased 42%" at Harris and "adjusted EBIT increased approximately 68% to $41 million"; management added "we expect Harris' price to moderate from first quarter record levels" and "we expect the Harris segment will operate in the 19% to 20% margin range at current metal prices." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno)

Q&A

  • Bryan Blair, Oppenheimer: asked about cycle positioning and demand acceleration; President, CEO & Chairman Hedlund replied, "we're cautiously optimistic" and said the company wants "more consistency month-to-month," while noting Europe "choppiness" and uncertainty in the Middle East.
  • Bryan Blair, Oppenheimer: asked on automation growth cadence and quoting; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno said, "we do expect to turn to modest growth on the automation side as we exit Q2" and expects second-half "broad volume improvement" with "order intake...strong" and "backlog levels strong."
  • Oliver Z Jiang, Morgan Stanley: asked what drove high-30% general fabrication growth and how it translates to back-half volumes; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno said "volumes, particularly on consumables in the Americas Welding segment were up low double digits" and tied the setup to "3 months in a row on PMI improving."
  • Mircea Dobre, Baird: pressed on price/cost timing and embedded pricing plus Harris moderation; President, CEO & Chairman Hedlund said, "I would expect that we're going to recover most of that in Q2" and Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno added, "I would expect price/cost neutral as we enter the third quarter" and described the updated price assumption as partly from "new price actions" and partly from Harris.
  • Walter Liptak, Seaport: asked why international margins are lower; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno pointed to volume/project timing and said, "depending on how the conflict progresses in the Middle East, we'll continue to see an impact there."
  • Christian Zyla, KeyBanc: asked on margin cadence and drivers; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno said, "I expect the second quarter to show a step improvement" and linked the back half to "volumes" with improving mix as 2025 price actions anniversary.
  • Andres Loret de Mola, Stifel: asked about cost management and Harris margin drivers; President, CEO & Chairman Hedlund cited "enterprise-led initiatives" including "sourcing," "supply chain planning," and "SG&A productivity initiatives," while Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno said Harris benefited from "mix," "pricing," and "leverage on SG&A."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on price/cost timing, international volume/margins, automation profitability, and margin cadence (e.g., "do you expect to be back to neutral from a price/cost standpoint in Q2?" (Mircea Dobre, Baird)).
  • Management: slightly positive but guarded, repeatedly using "cautiously optimistic" (President, CEO & Chairman Hedlund) while providing specific cadence language on pricing, volumes, and margin improvement (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno: "price/cost neutral as we enter the third quarter").
  • Versus last quarter: management maintained the same caution framing on recovery, with continued emphasis on PMI/order signals, but added conflict-specific quantified sales impacts and highlighted May price actions; analysts’ focus shifted more toward near-term price/cost recapture and international profitability tied to the Middle East.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language tightened on sales growth: Q4 described 2026 as "mid-single-digit percent" sales growth (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno), while Q1 updated to "high single-digit percent" due to "recently announced price actions" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno).
  • The Middle East moved from broader geopolitics context to an explicit quantified headwind: Q1 cited "$8 million to $10 million per quarter" sales impact (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno).
  • Automation commentary remained focused on order strength and second-half improvement, but Q1 emphasized near-term cadence: "turn to modest growth...as we exit Q2" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno), aligning with Q4’s expectation that automation sales would "pivot to growth starting in the second quarter" (President, CEO & Chairman Hedlund).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Heightened operating complexity from geopolitics and evolving trade negotiations" and "unfavorable" price/cost were key execution risks, with mitigation via "new price actions" effective in early May. (President, CEO & Chairman Hedlund)
  • The company cited the Middle East conflict as a direct demand headwind: "$8 million to $10 million per quarter" sales impact and uncertainty on duration; management said it is "monitoring for consistency" in EMEA orders, including possible "prebuying" dynamics. (President, CEO & Chairman Hedlund; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno)
  • Working capital/inventory risk was acknowledged as temporary: "We expect to reduce inventory levels in the second half of the year" and reiterated a target of "100% cash conversion." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bruno)

Final Takeaway

Lincoln Electric’s management described Q1 as delivering "record quarterly sales and adjusted EPS" while acknowledging that price/cost was unfavorable and that a return to neutrality is expected with May pricing actions reaching full effect in Q3. The company raised its 2026 net sales growth assumption to a "high single-digit percent" range, while quantifying an ongoing Middle East conflict impact of "$8 million to $10 million per quarter" and positioning for modest Americas volume growth beginning in Q2, supported by strengthening order rates and backlog trends.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: TechnipFMC targets $10B Subsea orders in 2026 while projecting 23% Q2 Subsea EBITDA margin
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TechnipFMC (FTI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Our quarterly results reflect strong operational performance throughout the company, driven by solid execution." (CEO & Executive Chairman Douglas Pferdehirt)
  • "Total company revenue in the period was $2.5 billion." (CEO & Executive Chairman Douglas Pferdehirt) "Adjusted EBITDA was $453 million with a margin of 18.2% when excluding foreign exchange." (CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt) "Free cash flow was $277 million with total shareholder distributions of $285 million in the quarter." (CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt)
  • "Orders in the quarter were $1.9 billion" in Subsea, and management said it "see[s] a strengthening trend in order activity as we move through the year, supporting our confidence in achieving $10 billion of Subsea orders in 2026." (CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt)
  • "As a reminder, only 4% of our revenue is derived from the Middle East." (CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt) "We were able to operate safely with minimal disruption." (CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt)
  • "With our quarterly update, the list now identifies approximately $30 billion of opportunities for potential award over the next 24 months, representing the seventh consecutive quarterly increase in value." (CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt)
  • "We remain committed to returning at least 70% of free cash flow to shareholders through both dividends and share repurchases." (CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt)
  • "Revenue in the quarter was $2.5 billion, adjusted EBITDA was $453 million when excluding a foreign exchange gain of $13 million." (Executive VP & CFO Alf Melin)

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter, we expect Subsea revenue to increase high single digits sequentially, with adjusted EBITDA margin improving approximately 300 basis points to 23%." (Executive VP & CFO Melin)
  • "For Surface Technologies, we anticipate revenue to decline low single digits sequentially with an adjusted EBITDA margin of approximately 17%." (Executive VP & CFO Melin) "[W]e expect corporate expense to decline approximately 25% in the second quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Melin)
  • "We remain very confident in our ability to exceed $2.1 billion of total company EBITDA in 2026." (Executive VP & CFO Melin) "[T]he operational momentum and improving commercial backdrop gives us high confidence in our ability to deliver continued growth in 2027." (Executive VP & CFO Melin)

Financial Results

  • "In Subsea, revenue of $2.2 billion increased 1% versus the fourth quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Melin) "Adjusted EBITDA was $441 million" and "Adjusted EBITDA margin improved to 20%." (Executive VP & CFO Melin)
  • "In Surface Technologies, revenue was $284 million, a decrease of 12% from the fourth quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Melin) "Adjusted EBITDA was $50 million" and "Adjusted EBITDA margin was 17.4%." (Executive VP & CFO Melin)
  • "Cash flow from operating activities was $332 million, with capital expenditures totaling $56 million in the quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Melin) "This resulted in free cash flow of $277 million." (Executive VP & CFO Melin)
  • "We repurchased $265 million of stock in the first quarter when including $20 million of dividends." (Executive VP & CFO Melin) "We ended the quarter with a net cash position of $540 million." (Executive VP & CFO Melin)

Q&A

  • John Anderson, Barclays: asked whether recent events are accelerating deepwater FIDs and customer confidence; CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt said "we are in discussions on opportunities to accelerate both brownfield tiebacks as well as some greenfield developments" and added "If you complement that with a higher commodity price, yes, clearly, those project economics look increasingly interesting to our clients."
  • John Anderson, Barclays: asked what portion of 2027 revenue could be Subsea 2.0 and margin implications; CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt said "I would put it in the neighborhood of about 50%, perhaps a bit north of 50%" and added "80% of our new orders are coming in with Subsea 2.0."
  • Scott Gruber, Citi: asked about constraints (engineers, vessels, capacity) if growth pulls forward; CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt said reducing cycle time "means we can do more with the same" and "continue to grow without being constrained or without having to have a big significant CapEx expenditure."
  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: pressed for details on SURF 2.0; CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt said "we are in the concept select stage" and "I really need to be careful not to go much further than that right now."
  • Marc Bianchi, TD Cowen: asked about unannounced Subsea awards and order composition; CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt said Q1 was "a very strong quarter given the fact that the amount of announced awards" was limited and added "there was a large project that we will be announcing once the customer gives us the permission."
  • Marc Bianchi, TD Cowen: asked why Surface trends were down despite North America strength; Executive VP & CFO Melin said "backlog scheduling in the Middle East is a big portion of the answer" and added "you should probably, at this point, expect that the revenue will be slightly lower compared to full year guidance" but "we are seeing an opportunity for stronger margin performance."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive but probing on whether a stronger deepwater cycle could create execution constraints and whether Surface weakness signaled conflict impact, including "Do you anticipate any constraints" (Scott Gruber, Citi) and "I was surprised" (Marc Bianchi, TD Cowen).
  • Management tone was positive and confident on Subsea growth and 2026 targets, using phrases such as "we remain very confident" and "high confidence" while being guarded on SURF 2.0 specifics: "I don't want to get ahead of them." (CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt)
  • Versus Q4 2025, the Q1 2026 discussion added a more explicit geopolitical frame (Middle East) and more frequent emphasis on near-term acceleration conversations, while retaining the same core message on cycle time, direct awards, and a strengthening Subsea opportunity set.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 introduced conflict-related operating context and reiterated limited exposure: "only 4% of our revenue is derived from the Middle East" (CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt), while Q4 2025 focused more on portfolio approach adoption and greenfield momentum.
  • Subsea opportunity list increased to "approximately $30 billion" and a "seventh consecutive quarterly increase" (CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt) vs. Q4 2025 at "approximately $29 billion" and a "sixth consecutive quarterly increase" (CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt).
  • Q1 2026 guidance commentary emphasized Q2 Subsea margin stepping to "23%" (Executive VP & CFO Melin); Q4 2025 emphasized updated full-year Subsea guidance and restructuring actions intended to "deliver sustainable improvements in 2026" (Executive VP & CFO Melin).

Risks and Concerns

  • "The well-being of our employees and their families is paramount" and the company took "immediate and comprehensive measures" in the Middle East, though management said it operated "with minimal disruption." (CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt)
  • Surface Technologies volatility was tied to activity timing: "scheduled timing of project-related activity in the Middle East" (Executive VP & CFO Melin), with management pointing to mix effects: "lower revenue but higher margin." (Executive VP & CFO Melin)
  • Supply chain readiness was addressed as growth ramps: CEO & Executive Chairman Pferdehirt said Subsea 2.0 "significantly reduced our reliance upon the supply chain" and enables annual planning with suppliers by quantities rather than bespoke specs.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as an operationally strong start to 2026, anchored by $2.5 billion in revenue, $277 million of free cash flow, and continued shareholder returns, while reiterating confidence in $10 billion of Subsea orders and exceeding $2.1 billion of total company EBITDA for 2026. Leadership tied the 2027 growth narrative to a rising $30 billion Subsea opportunity list, higher Subsea 2.0 adoption (with an expectation that roughly half of 2027 revenue could be tied to Subsea 2.0), and ongoing customer discussions to accelerate brownfield tiebacks and select greenfield projects, alongside a stated focus on cycle-time reduction as the primary driver of capacity and margin expansion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: uniQure rises after regulatory updates on gene therapy
data.description:
uniQure (QURE) shares jumped ~22% on Thursday after the Dutch biotech announced positive regulatory developments related to its gene therapy candidate AMT-130 at a time when its U.S. approval remains uncertain.

The company disclosed plans to submit a marketing authorization application in Q3 2026, seeking the U.K. approval of AMT-130 for Huntington’s disease, a rare genetically driven neurodegenerative disorder.

The decision followed a positive presubmission meeting conducted with the U.K.'s Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency, the company said. “We are encouraged by the constructive feedback from the MHRA as we continue to work towards a regulatory submission for AMT-130 in Huntington’s disease,” CEO Matt Kapusta remarked.

Additionally, uniQure (QURE) said that the U.S. FDA has granted a Type B meeting for the company. At the meeting scheduled for Q2 2026, the company intends to discuss a proposed Phase 3 trial decision for AMT-130 and receive regulatory feedback on a data analysis plan for four-year results expected in Q3 2026.

QURE shares plunged in February after FDA Commissioner Marty Makary, in a potential reference to AMT-130, suggested that his agency would not approve drugs that are associated with patient morbidity.

data.title: Apple's Cook, Meta's Zuckerberg among rumored bidders for Seattle Seahawks: report
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) CEO Tim Cook and Meta Platforms (META) CEO and co-founder Mark Zuckerberg are among the rumored bidders for the NFL's Seattle Seahawks, Front Office Sports reported.

Zuckerberg is said to be considering a bid, the news outlet added, citing five sources familiar with the matter. Cook is also weighing an offer for the reigning Super Bowl champions, the news outlet added, citing four sources.

At press time, it was not clear if either Cook or Zuckerberg had made a formal offer. 

Apple and Meta did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Cook announced earlier this month that he would be stepping down as Apple's CEO by Sept. 1, with John Ternus as his replacement. The 65-year-old Cook, who will remain as Apple's Executive Chairman, has a deep passion for sports, specifically college sports. On social media, Cook, who did his undergrad at Auburn University and received a master's degree from Duke University, has routinely cheered on his former schools. 

Zuckerberg has also expressed an interest in sports and has incorporated Brazilian jiu-jitsu and martial arts training into his exercise routines. He has also had a hand in Facebook broadcasting live MLB games.

Seahawks sale history

The Seahawks are up for sale as a result after former owner Paul Allen passed away in 2018. Allen, the co-founder of Microsoft (MSFT), stipulated in his estate, the Paul G. Allen estate, that his sports holdings, which included the Seahawks, the NBA's Portland Trail Blazers, and Major League Soccer's Seattle Sounders (of which he was a co-owner), were to be eventually sold. The proceeds are set to be given to various philanthropic efforts.

Allen's sister, Jody, currently has control of the Seahawks. She also is the co-founder and president of the Paul G. Allen Family Foundation and is executor of Allen's estate. 

The Trail Blazers were officially sold in September 2025 to a group of investors, led by Carolina Hurricanes owner Tom Dundon. The sale was officially approved in March 2026 by the NBA's board of governors, ESPN reported.

In February, the Paul G. Allen estate said it had begun the sale process of the Seahawks, days after winning Super Bowl LX, a 29-13 win over the New England Patriots, according to the team's official website.

The sale is being handled by investment bank Allen & Co. and law firm Latham & Watkins. Some estimates have suggested that the Seahawks could fetch a price of more than $6.05B, the now-record price tag that private equity titan Josh Harris paid for the Washington Commanders in 2023.

Allen paid $200M for the Seahawks in 1997.

Allen & Co. and Latham & Watkins did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Cook is believed to have a net worth of around $3B, according to Forbes, while Zuckerberg has a net worth of more than $200B, making him the fifth richest person in the world.

data.title: Polen Global Growth adds META, ASML; exits ABT, ADBE among Q1 moves
data.description:
The Polen Global Growth Composite Portfolio lagged the Index in the first quarter, as the portfolio slipped 15.7% net of fees, while the MSCI ACWI Index declined 3.2%.

The fund, in its quarterly letter, said that the top relative contributors to the portfolio's performance included TSMC (TSM), Tesla (not owned), and Apple (not owned). By contrast, the largest relative detractors in the quarter were Adyen, Boston Scientific, and CoStar Group.

In the first quarter, the fund initiated new positions in Siemens Energy (SMNEY), Tokyo Electron (TOELY), Meta Platforms (META), and ASML (ASML).

The fund also strengthened its holdings in Boston Scientific (BSX), Spotify (SPOT), Tencent Holdings (TCEHY), Broadcom (AVGO), NVIDIA (NVDA), Shopify (SHOP), ServiceNow (NOW), and CoStar Group (CSGP).

On the other hand, the portfolio exited its positions in Abbott Laboratories (ABT), Adobe (ADBE), Paycom (PAYC), and SAP (SAP) to redeploy the capital to its highest-conviction software names, such as ServiceNow, Shopify, and CoStar Group.

The fund also trimmed its exposure to Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), MSCI Inc., Oracle (ORCL), Microsoft (MSFT), and Siemens Healthineers (SMMNY).

Source: Q1 fund letter

data.title: Camping World reiterates $275M-$325M adjusted EBITDA outlook as it targets SG&A and inventory reductions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Camping World Holdings (CWH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Matt Wagner said the quarter showed cost discipline and operating leverage despite weak demand: "despite a challenging RV industry backdrop, we delivered a first quarter that demonstrates the discipline and operating leverage we discussed on our last call" (President, CEO & Director Matt Wagner).
  • Wagner framed the quarter around gaining unit share, lowering costs, and scaling Good Sam, highlighting cost execution: "we reduced SG&A by more than $29 million or 7.5%" and "the 135 basis point improvement in SG&A to gross profit and the $29 million reduction reflects a fundamentally lower cost basis, not onetime savings" (President, CEO & Director Wagner).
  • On sales mix and market share, Wagner emphasized exclusive brands, including Fifth Wheel performance: "Within the new Fifth Wheel segment, we're up nearly 10% year-to-date, driven by the introduction of private label products" (President, CEO & Director Wagner).
  • CFO Tom Kirn detailed margin pressure tied to aging inventory actions and reiterated the timeline: "vehicle gross margins were under pressure in the first quarter as we moved through assets in certain aging buckets" and "We expect this gross margin trend to continue through the second quarter... before beginning to improve in the back half of 2026" (Chief Financial & Accounting Officer Thomas Kirn).

Outlook

  • Wagner reiterated full-year profitability guidance while narrowing the company’s view on industry conditions: "we believe the new RV industry is likely tracking towards the lower end of our 2026 retail outlook, calling for 325,000 to 350,000 units" and "Today, we are reiterating our full year 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance range of $275 million to $325 million" (President, CEO & Director Matt Wagner).
  • Kirn pointed to improving Good Sam margin trajectory across 2026: "We believe Good Sam margins should show year-over-year improvements through the balance of the year" (Chief Financial & Accounting Officer Kirn).
  • Management did not provide EPS or revenue guidance figures in the prepared remarks or Q&A.

Financial Results

  • Kirn reported Q1 revenue and profitability: "For the first quarter, we recorded revenue of $1.35 billion" and "Our first quarter adjusted EBITDA of $28 million compares to $31.2 million in the first quarter of 2025" (Chief Financial & Accounting Officer Thomas Kirn).
  • Kirn quantified the vehicle margin pressure: "New vehicle gross margin declined 148 basis points to 12.2% and used vehicle gross margin declined 91 basis points to 17.7%" (Chief Financial & Accounting Officer Kirn).
  • Kirn highlighted liquidity and deleveraging progress: "We ended the quarter with $200 million of cash on the balance sheet" and "We also paid down $56 million of debt in the quarter" (Chief Financial & Accounting Officer Kirn).

Q&A

  • Patrick Buckley, Jefferies: asked about higher F&I per unit and outlook; President Wagner tied it to down-payment mix and attachment of add-on products: "we're still seeing a higher product attachment... all the Good Sam affinity products that we offer" (President, CEO & Director Wagner).
  • Buckley, Jefferies: asked about used ASP decline and negative equity; President Wagner said the ASP move was minor and described the equity backdrop: "We're not seeing that negative equity trend being amplified" and "We're seeing more of a corrective self-healing environment in this industry" (President, CEO & Director Wagner).
  • James Hardiman, Citigroup: asked to separate weather vs. Middle East impacts; President Wagner cited store closures and stabilization into April: "we had to shut down in excess of 60 of our stores" and "we are trending to be positive on a same-store basis" (President, CEO & Director Wagner).
  • Joseph Altobello, Raymond James: asked about turnover timing and whether the $35 million EBITDA inventory-turn impact remains; President Wagner emphasized annualized measurement and pacing: "you should be looking at those turnover goals on an annualized basis" (President, CEO & Director Wagner).
  • Altobello, Raymond James: asked about Costco partnership contribution; President Wagner said it remains outside guidance and described a reset: "It's not" and "we actually took a little bit of a pause for a moment" (President, CEO & Director Wagner).
  • Tristan Thomas-Martin, BMO: asked about trade-in cycle evidence and 2027 pricing; President Wagner said trade-ins are early and cited early model-year price indications: "we've not yet seen a material increase in trade-in percentages" and "we're only seeing about a 1% to 2% price increase" (President, CEO & Director Wagner).
  • Scott Stember, ROTH: asked about parts/service mix and capacity; President Wagner said used reconditioning still consumes capacity while external service growth is a focus: "The narrative still remains relatively the same" (President, CEO & Director Wagner).
  • Stember, ROTH: asked about free cash flow and leverage expectations; CFO Kirn outlined the cash framework: "Our goal this year in terms of net CapEx is to be south of $100 million" (Chief Financial & Accounting Officer Thomas Kirn).
  • Andrew Didora, BofA: pressed on the SG&A runway and AI/IT savings; President Wagner avoided a target but cited an internal CRM build as a cost proof point: "I'm not going to give a specific range yet" and "we were able to stand up that entire environment with 3 individuals" (President, CEO & Director Wagner).
  • Alice Wycklendt, Baird: asked about consumer credit; CFO Kirn said underwriting trends are stable, and Brett Andress cited improving rate quotes: "we've not seen any sort of different behaviors" and "we have actually seen rates start to drift down" (Chief Financial & Accounting Officer Thomas Kirn; Senior Vice President of Corporate Development & Investor Relations Brett Andress).
  • Wycklendt, Baird: asked about locations and M&A; President Wagner disclosed a small acquisition and disciplined brand-focused consolidation: "last month, we did close on an acquisition" and "we've taken that latter position" (President, CEO & Director Wagner).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, focusing on weather volatility, guidance durability, inventory-turn execution, Costco ramp, SG&A headroom, and consumer credit; Hardiman’s question referenced "a lot of moving pieces" and asked if the company would be "raising today" absent geopolitical disruption (James Hardiman, Citigroup).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A; Wagner repeatedly emphasized control levers and stabilization, including: "we can control much more of our SG&A structure" (President, CEO & Director Matt Wagner).
  • Compared with last quarter, management remained consistent on the same three priorities (share, SG&A, Good Sam) and maintained the same adjusted EBITDA range, while Q&A in Q1 leaned more toward execution details (Costco mechanics, AI cost takeout examples, lender rate drift).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was unchanged quarter over quarter: "we are reiterating our full year 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance range of $275 million to $325 million" (President, CEO & Director Matt Wagner) vs. Q4’s "adjusted EBITDA range of $275 million to $325 million for the full year 2026" (President, CEO & Director Wagner).
  • Inventory-cleansing-driven margin pressure remained a central message, with Q1 CFO guidance on timing consistent with Q4: "We expect this gross margin trend to continue through the second quarter" (Chief Financial & Accounting Officer Thomas Kirn) vs. Q4’s "We expect this margin pressure to persist during the first half of 2026" (CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Thomas Kirn).
  • Messaging shifted toward demonstrated savings and deleveraging execution in Q1; Q4 discussed planned savings and dividend pause, while Q1 emphasized realized actions: "we reduced SG&A by more than $29 million" and "We also paid down $56 million of debt" (President, CEO & Director Matt Wagner; Chief Financial & Accounting Officer Thomas Kirn).

Risks and Concerns

  • Wagner said demand and operational disruptions weighed on results, including weather-driven store closures: "we had to shut down in excess of 60 of our stores" (President, CEO & Director Matt Wagner).
  • Kirn flagged continued near-term margin pressure tied to aging inventory: "We expect this gross margin trend to continue through the second quarter" (Chief Financial & Accounting Officer Thomas Kirn).
  • Wagner described a promotional industry environment as a constraint on pricing: "there is quite a promotional environment that exists out there" (President, CEO & Director Matt Wagner).

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that Q1 execution was centered on share gains from exclusive brands, structural SG&A reduction, Good Sam margin stabilization, and balance-sheet improvement, while reiterating full-year 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $275 million to $325 million and acknowledging that vehicle gross margin pressure is expected to persist into Q2 before improving in the back half of 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Steve Eisman says he sees no issue with market right now
data.description:
“Big Short” investor Steve Eisman said Thursday he remains bullish on the stock market (SPY) despite recent volatility and concerns about valuations.

In an interview on CNBC, the former Neuberger senior portfolio manager dismissed talk of a market regime change, noting that the same leaders from before a brief March selloff have returned to the top. Tech stocks (VGT) (IYW) (XLK), banks (KBWB) (BNKS) (KRE) and power-related industrials remain his preferred holdings.

“I don’t have a problem with the market right now,” Eisman said. “It’s amazing. I really don’t.”

When asked about concerns that the market is expensive by traditional metrics, Eisman pointed to the shifting composition of the S&P 500 (SPY). He noted that information technology now represents 35% of the index, up from 18% to 20% a decade ago. Including Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Meta (META) pushes that figure to roughly 50%.

“The market’s more expensive because tech has become a much bigger percentage of the S&P,” he said. “Tech sells at higher multiples generally than everything else.”

On private credit (VPC) (HYIN), Eisman acknowledged problems but dismissed systemic risk, citing banks’ strong capital positions. He expressed concern about private equity’s overexposure to software (IGV) companies, whose valuations have plummeted amid AI disruption jitters.

Elsewhere in the wide-ranging interview, Eisman revealed he is shorting Fair Isaac (FICO), criticizing the company for raising prices roughly 500% over many years.

data.title: AlzChem Group AG GAAP EPS of $1.77, revenue of $148.7M
data.description:
• AlzChem Group AG press release (ALZCF): Q1 GAAP EPS of $1.77.
  • Revenue of $148.7M.
